elements.sent-2411-001: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a promising method to learn optimal control policies for systems with unknown dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, synthesizing controllers for safety-critical systems based on high-level specifications, such as those expressed in temporal languages like linear temporal logic (LTL), presents a significant challenge in control systems research.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current RL-based methods designed for LTL tasks typically offer only asymptotic guarantees, which provide no insight into the transient performance during the learning phase.
elements.sent-2411-004: While running an RL algorithm, it is crucial to assess how close we are to achieving optimal behavior if we stop learning.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we present the first regret-free online algorithm for learning a controller that addresses the general class of LTL specifications over Markov decision processes (MDPs) with a finite set of states and actions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We begin by proposing a regret-free learning algorithm to solve infinite-horizon reach-avoid problems.
elements.sent-2411-007: For general LTL specifications, we show that the synthesis problem can be reduced to a reach-avoid problem when the graph structure is known.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we provide an algorithm for learning the graph structure, assuming knowledge of a minimum transition probability, which operates independently of the main regret-free algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-009: Reinforcement learning (RL) is becoming more prevalent in computing efficient policies for systems with unknown dynamical models.
elements.sent-2411-010: Although experimental evidence highlights the effectiveness of RL in numerous applications, ensuring the safety and reliability of algorithms is imperative for certain safety-critical systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: In such scenarios, the development of algorithms that provide explicit performance guarantees becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-012: Among the existing performance guarantees for RL, regret minimization has widely been studied recently \cite{Jacksh2011, Agarwal2014, Srinivas2012, Dann2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: For an online learning algorithm, intuitively, regret is defined as the difference between the accumulated (expected) rewards collected by an optimal policy and the the algorithm during learning.
elements.sent-2411-014: Existing regret minimization algorithms assume that optimal policies are positional, meaning that optimal policies (deterministically) map every state into a corresponding action.
elements.sent-2411-015: While this suffices for most of basic reward structures, optimal policies may not be positional for more general reward structures.
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, when the control objective is set by an LTL formula, optimal policy is, in general, not positional.
elements.sent-2411-017: RL for LTL specifications has recently become popular.
elements.sent-2411-018: Majority of the existing methods provide no performance guarantees \cite{Icarte2018, camacho2019, hasanbeig2019, kazemi2022, hahn2019, Oura2020, Cai2020, Bozkurt2021, Sickert2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Controller synthesis for finite-horizon and infinite-horizon LTL with probably approximately correct (PAC) guarantee has recently been studied \cite{Fu2014,Chaudhuri:2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: It is shown that providing PAC guarantees for learning algorithms that target controller synthesis against infinite-horizon specifications requires additional knowledge, such as minimum transition probability \cite{alur2022framework .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, there exists no regret-free RL-based controller synthesis method for LTL tasks.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we propose an online learning algorithm for control synthesis problems against LTL objectives, which provides sublinearly growing regret bounds.
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, we want to measure the performance of the learned control policy during learning.
elements.sent-2411-024: For that, we compare the satisfaction probabilities of an optimal policy and sequence of policies generated by the algorithm during learning.
elements.sent-2411-025: We consider the class of systems, whose dynamics can be captured by a finite MDP with unknown transition probabilities and fixed initial state $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The control objective is to synthesize a control policy that maximizes probability of satisfying a given LTL specification $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Let $\pi^\ast$ denote an optimal policy, meaning that applying $\pi^\ast$ maximizes the satisfaction probability for $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Running an online algorithm will produce a sequence of policies $\pi_1,\pi_2,\dots$, one per episode.
elements.sent-2411-029: Let $v^\ast(s_\init), v_k(s_\init)$ denote, respectively, the satisfaction probabilities of $\pi^\ast$ and $\pi_k$ when starting from $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-030: After passing $K>0$ episodes, regret is defined as $R(k)\coloneqq \sum_{k=1 ^K (v^\ast(s_\init) - v_k(s_\init))$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The algorithm we propose in this paper is regret free, meaning that $\lim_{K\rightarrow \infty R(K)/K=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: There exist regret minimization techniques for solving infinite-horizon controller synthesis problems.
elements.sent-2411-033: On one hand, regret-free algorithms such as UCRL2 \cite{Jacksh2011 can only be applied to communicative MDPs; for infinite-horizon specifications such as reach-avoid, the representative MDP will be alwayse non-communicating.}
elements.sent-2411-034: On the other hand, there exist online no-regret algorithms that can be applied to stochastic shortest path (SSP), which is closely related to the infinite-horizon reachability problem in LTL \cite{Tarbouriech:2019, rosenberg2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: There are multiple obstacles which prohibit direct application of those methods to our setting.
elements.sent-2411-036: First, the primary outcome we seek is the regret value with respect to an optimal policy's satisfaction probability, rather than regret based on an arbitrary cost structure.
elements.sent-2411-037: Any cost structure suitable for applying standard SSP algorithms restricts costs to strictly positive values.
elements.sent-2411-038: This restriction prevents a formulation that computes the accumulated regret relative to the optimal satisfaction probability, which is our main objective.
elements.sent-2411-039: Second, one main assumption for the proposed methods attacking the SSP problem is to assume that there exists at least one policy under which the target is reachable with probability $1$.
elements.sent-2411-040: To relax this assumption, existing methods often impose strong requirements, such as knowing an upper bound on the total accumulated cost and having sufficiently high cost assignments for visiting non-accepting end components.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our formulation avoids all of these assumptions and only requires knowledge of the minimum transition probability in the MDP.
elements.sent-2411-042: We first state our algorithm for reach-avoid specifications, wherein a set of goal states must be visited before hitting a set of bad states.
elements.sent-2411-043: In every episode, our algorithm (1) computes an interval MDP (iMDP) by taking all of the collected observations into account; (2) finds an optimistic policy over the computed iMDP to solve the target reach-avoid problem; (3) executes the computed policy before an episode-specific deadline reached.
elements.sent-2411-044: We prove that the regret of our algorithm grows sublinearly with respect to the number of episodes.
elements.sent-2411-045: For a general LTL specification, we show that the synthesis problem can be reduced to a reach-avoid problem, if we know the connection graph of the MDP.
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally, we provide a polynomial algorithm for learning the graph structure of MDPs.
elements.sent-2411-047: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: in Sec.~\ref{sec:related , we discuss the existing related literature; in Sec.~\ref{sec:prelims , we provide the required notations and definitions; in Sec.~\ref{sec:reach-avoid , we describe our algorithm for the reach-avoid problem and explain derivation of our claimed regret bounds; in Sec.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We discuss existing results in four related domains.
elements.sent-2411-049: Non-Guaranteed reinforcement learning techniques for LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-050: During the course of the past years, lots of efforts have been dedicated into solving the controller synthesis problem for systems modeled as finite MDPs with unknown transition probabilities and have to fulfill certain tasks encoded as formula in LTL.
elements.sent-2411-051: Early results focused only on the finite-horizon subset of LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-052: Icarte et al. introduced reward machines, which use finite state automata to encode finite-horizon specifications, along with specialized (deep) Q-learning algorithms to support their approach \cite{Icarte2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Camacho et al. later formalized the automatic derivation of reward machines for finite-horizon subsets of LTL \cite{camacho2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The development of efficient and compact automata, such as limit deterministic Büchi automata (LDBA), for representing LTL formulas has led to significant advances in reinforcement learning for the full class of LTL specifications, including infinite-horizon cases \cite{Sickert2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Typically, one has to first translate the given LTL formula into an appropriate automaton, such as a Rabin or limit-deterministic Büchi automaton, and then compute the product of this automaton with the actual MDP to formulate the final (discounted) learning problem.
elements.sent-2411-056: This formulation ensures that, with a sufficiently large discount factor (dependent on system dynamics and goal specifications), applying standard RL techniques will lead the policy to converge asymptotically to the optimal one \cite{kazemi2022, Bozkurt2021, Oura2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, these methods fail to provide a finite-time performance, and the critical lower bound for the discount factor cannot be known in advance.
elements.sent-2411-058: Guaranteed reinforcement learning for LTL specification The two most popular metrics for evaluating the performance of learning algorithms are probably approximately correct (PAC) and regret bounds.
elements.sent-2411-059: In \cite{Fu2014 , the problem of synthesizing controllers for finite-horizon LTL specifications over finite MDPs with unknown transition probabilities was addressed, and a PAC learning algorithm was proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, its sample complexity explicitly depends on the horizon length, making it unsuitable for full LTL with infinite-horizon specifications.
elements.sent-2411-061: A few years later, a surprising negative result emerged: full LTL is not PAC learnable \cite{yang2021tractability, alur2022framework .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Upon closer examination, the main issue behind this result is the assumption of an unknown minimum transition probability.
elements.sent-2411-063: In \cite{Chaudhuri:2023 , by assuming the minimum transition probability is known, a PAC-learnable controller synthesis was proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, their approach requires access to a generative model, allowing data collection from any arbitrary state-action pair.
elements.sent-2411-065: In many realistic scenarios, this is impractical since initializing the MDP at arbitrary states is not feasible.
elements.sent-2411-066: Our proposed algorithm, however, does not require access to a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-067: Recently, \cite{Mateo:2023 proposed another PAC learning algorithm that also does not rely on a generative model.}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, there is still no regret-free online algorithm for controller synthesis with LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-069: Regret-free reinforcement learning for communicating MDPs UCRL and UCRL2 \cite{Jacksh2011, Auer2007 are well-known regret-free learning algorithms proposed for communicating MDPs.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The way regret is defined for communicating MDPs makes them particularly suitable for our objective: regret is measured over an infinite sequence of states without discounting later observations, which aligns perfectly with the requirements of infinite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-071: Notably, learning in communicating MDPs can continue indefinitely without the need for resetting.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, we observe that in the problem we aim to solve, even if the underlying system's MDP is communicating, its product with the automaton modeling the specification may become non-communicating \cite{kazemi2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: In our case, the initial state is fixed and located within a transient subset, making algorithms like UCRL2, which are designed for communicating MDPs, inapplicable.
elements.sent-2411-074: Regret-free reinforcement learning for non-communicating MDPs Goal-oriented reinforcement learning is a key class of problems in RL, often formulated as a shortest path problem (SPP) for MDPs with unknown transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-075: Recently, several exciting theoretical results have been published \cite{Tarbouriech:2019, rosenberg2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In particular, the online learning algorithm proposed by \cite{Tarbouriech:2019 provides sub-linear regret bounds for the accumulated cost in MDPs, assuming (1) the existence of a proper policy that reaches the goal with probability one, and (2) all costs are positive.}
elements.sent-2411-077: They argue that (1) can be relaxed if an upper bound on the accumulated cost is known, and (2) by perturbing the costs.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, the assumption of a maximum accumulated cost is often unrealistic, and we are more interested in computing regret bounds over the probability of satisfaction, rather than the accumulated cost.
elements.sent-2411-079: For a matrix $X\in\reals^{m\times n}$, we define the $\infty$-matrix-norm $\|X\|_\infty\coloneqq \max_{1\leq i \leq m}\sum_{j=1 ^n\|X_{ij}\|$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: We consider specifications in the form of formulas in Linear Temporal Logic (LTL).
elements.sent-2411-082: Here, we give a brief introduction to LTL.
elements.sent-2411-083: For detailed syntax and semantics of LTL, we refer to the book by Baier and Katoen \cite{baier2008principles and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-084: We consider LTL specifications with syntax \begin{equation}\psi := p \,|\, \neg \psi \,|\,\psi_1 \wedge \psi_2 \,|\, \mathord{\bigcirc} \psi \,|\, \psi_1\mathbin{\sf U} \psi_2,\end{equation} where $p\subset S$ is an element of the set of atomic propositions $AP$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Let $\rho=\rho_0,\rho_1,\dots$ be an infinite sequence of elements from $2^{AP}$ and denote $\rho_i=\rho_i,\rho_{i+1 ,\dots$ for any $i\in \nats$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Then the satisfaction relation between $\rho$ and a property $\psi$, expressed in LTL, is denoted by $\rho\models\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-090: The temporal until operator $\rho\models \psi_1\mathbin{\sf U}\psi_2$ holds if $ \exists i \in \mathbb{N :$ $\rho_{i \models \psi_2, \mbox{and \forall j \in{\mathbb{N : 0\leq j<i, \rho_{j}\models \psi_1 $.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The operator $\rho\models\LTLeventually \psi$ is used to denote that the property will eventually happen at some point in the future.
elements.sent-2411-093: The operator $\rho\models\LTLalways \psi$ signifies that $\psi$ must always be true at all times in the future.
elements.sent-2411-095: For a given LTL specification $\p$, we can monitor satisfaction of $\p$ by running an appropriate automaton $\Ap =(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_\init, F)$, which consists of a finite set of states $Q$, a finite alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$, a transition function $\delta\colon Q\times \Sigma \mapsto 2^Q$, an initial state $q_\init$, and an accepting condition $Acc$.
elements.sent-2411-096: For example, the accepting condition in deterministic Rabin automaton (DRA) is in the form of tuples ${(J_i,K_i)\mid i=1,\dots,m}$, consisting of subsets $J_i$ and $K_i$ of $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-097: An infinite sequence $\rho$ is accepted by the DRA $\Ap$ if there exists at least one pair $(J_i , K_i) \in Acc$ such that $\inf(\rho) \cap J_i = \emptyset$ and $\inf(\rho) \cap K_i \neq \emptyset$, where $\inf(\rho)$ is the set of states that appear infinitely often in $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Let $\Delta(X)$ be the set of probability distributions over the set $X$ and $AP$ be a set of atomic propositions.
elements.sent-2411-099: We consider MDPs $\M=(S, A, T, s_\init, AP)$, where $S$ and $A$ denote the finite set of states and actions, respectively, $T\colon S\times A\times S\rightarrow \Delta(S)$ denotes an unknown transition function, $s_\init\in S$ is the initial state of the MDP and $AP$ is the set of atomic propositions.
elements.sent-2411-100: We denote labeling function by $L\colon S\rightarrow 2^{AP}$.
elements.sent-2411-101: Let $\Pi$ denote the set of all deterministic positional policies over $\M$, that is $\pi\colon S\rightarrow A$ for $\pi \in \Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-102: By fixing a specific policy $\pi\in \Pi$, the MDP reduces into a Markov chain $\C=(S,P,s_\init, AP)$.
elements.sent-2411-103: For a given MDP $\M$, maximal end components (MECs) are sub-MDPs which are probabilistically closed, that is, (1) there exists a positional policy under which one can travel between every pair of states within the end component with probability $1$, and (2) the end component cannot be exited under any positional policy.
elements.sent-2411-104: An MDP is called communicating if it is comprised of only one MEC including every state.
elements.sent-2411-105: For an MDP $\M$, the underlying graph is denoted as $\chi_\M=(S,A,E)$, where $\chi_\M\subseteq S\times A\times S$ is defined such that $(s,a,s') \in E$ if and only if $T(s,a,s')>0$.
elements.sent-2411-106: We are interested in synthesizing policies that maximize satisfaction probability of LTL specifications over MDPs with finite set of states and actions and fixed initial state $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-107: Let $v^\ast(s_\init)$ denote the probability with which the optimal policy $\pi^\ast$ satisfies the target specification $\p$, when started at $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-108: Learning takes place over consecutive episodes.
elements.sent-2411-109: For the episode $k$ we define $v_k(s_\init)$ denote the probability of satisfying the target specification $\p$ under the policy $\pi_k$ in the MDP $\Mp$, when started at $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-110: We measure the success of the learning algorithm through its corresponding regret that is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:regret-def} R(K)\coloneqq \sum_{k=1}^{K}(v^\ast(s_\init)-v_{k}(s_\init)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-111: In practice, one is interested to terminate the online algorithm based on a reasonable stopping criterion.
elements.sent-2411-112: Let us define the normalized regret, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:normalized_regret} R_a (K)= \frac{R(K)}{K}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-113: An online algorithm is called regret-free if its corresponding regret grows sublinearly with respect to the number of episodes $K$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Running a regret-free online algorithm enables achieving arbitrary small values of normalized regret.
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, one could fix a threshold $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ and terminate the algorithm once the corresponding normalized regret goes below $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Therefore, one can consider the smallest number of episodes $k^\ast_{reg}\in \nats$ after which $R_a(k^\ast_{reg )<\varepsilon$ with confidence $1-\delta$ as a complexity metric for the proposed online learning algorithm, with respect to parameters $\delta,\varepsilon\in(0,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Intuitively, after $k>k^\ast_{reg}$ many learning episodes, with confidence $1-\delta$ the average satisfaction probability for the policy computed in the $k^{th}$ episode, will be $\varepsilon$-optimal.

elements.section-2411-004: Regret-Free Controller Synthesis for Reach-Avoid Specifications
elements.sent-2411-118: In this section, we study the controller synthesis problem for MDPs with unknown (but fixed) transition function against reach-avoid specifications.
elements.sent-2411-119: Let $G$ and $B$ be two distinct atomic propositions.
elements.sent-2411-120: We are interested in finding policies which can maximize the satisfaction probability for the (unbounded) reach-avoid specification $\p=\neg B\mathbin{\sf U G$.}
elements.sent-2411-121: We let $\Mp=(S,A,T,s_\init,\set{G, B )$ be the MDP that is resulted by making states within $G$ and $B$ absorbing.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Now, we are able to define the problem that is the main subject of study in this section.
elements.sent-2411-123: Our proposed algorithm is demonstrated in Alg.~\ref{alg:main .}
elements.sent-2411-124: We propose our algorithm in the known paradigm of optimism in the face of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-125: Learning takes place over consecutive environmental episodes.
elements.sent-2411-126: Each episode is a finite sequence $s_1, a_1, s_2,\dots s_L$ that starts from the initial state of $\Mp$, i.e., $s_1=s_\init$, and ends if either (1) one of the MECs in $G$ are reached meaning that $s_L\in G$, or (2) an episode-specific deadline is reached.. Computing confidence intervals.
elements.sent-2411-127: Let $\delta\in(0,1)$ be a given confidence threshold, $t_k$ be the time point at which $k^{th}$ episode begins and $N_k(s,a)$ denote the number of times the state-action pair $(s,a)$ has been visited before the start of the $k^{th}$ episode.
elements.sent-2411-128: Let $\Thk$ and $\Mbk$ denote the empirical transition function and the set of statistically plausible MDPs , both computed using the observations before the start of the $k^{th}$ episode.
elements.sent-2411-129: In particular, we define $\Mbk$ as the interval MDP with interval transition function $\Tbk$ for which all transition functions $\Tk\in\Tbk$ satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:trans-conf-bound} \| \Tk(\cdot\mid s,a)-\Thk(\cdot\mid s,a)\|_1\leq \beta_k(s,a)\eqqcolon \sqrt{\frac{8|S|\log(2|A|k/3\delta)}{\max{(1, N_k(s,a))}}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: Intuitively, we pick the confidence bound on the right hand side of Eq.~\eqref{eq:trans-conf-bound , such that the corresponding inequality holds with high confidence.}
elements.sent-2411-131: More concretely, we have the following result.
elements.sent-2411-133: In every episode $k\in\nats$, we use a modified version of extended value iteration (EVI) to compute an optimistic MDP $\Mtk\in\Mbk$ and an optimistic policy $\Pitk$ such that $\Pitk$ maximizes probability of reaching $G$ on the optimistic MDP $\Mtk$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Fixing the policy $\Pitk$ on $\Mtk$ induces a discrete-time Markov chain (DTMC) with transition function denoted by $\Ptk$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Alg.~\ref{alg:EVI illustrates our proposed extended value iteration (EVI) algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-136: One key input to our algorithm is a vector $\Lambda \in \reals_{>0 ^{|S|}$, whose entries contain an upper bound on the expected hitting time required to reach $G$ under an optimal policy $\pi^\ast$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The following lemma outlines the procedure for computing $\Lambda$, assuming it is finite.
elements.sent-2411-138: Let $\M'$ denote the MDP that is resulted by connecting $B$ to $s_\init$ in $\M$ with probability $1$, and $\lambda^\ast\in \reals_{>0 ^{|S|}$ denote the vector whose entries contain the expected time to hit $G$ in MDP $\M'$ under policy $\pi^\ast$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: With confidence $1-\delta$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lambda_bound} \lambda^\ast\leq \Lambda\coloneqq |S|\frac{\log(\delta)}{\log(1-\pmin^{|S|})},\end{equation} if $\lambda^\ast$ is finite, i.e., when $G$ is reachable with non-zero probability.
elements.sent-2411-140: One may notice that the input $B$ not only contains the states corresponding to the reach-avoid specification, but also includes every MEC whose intersection with $G$ is empty.
elements.sent-2411-141: In App.~\ref{app: graph-identification , we have discussed details of an algorithm for learning the MDP graph $\chi_{\M}$ up to any desired confidence, using the knowledge of the minimum transition probability $\pmin$.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Given $\chi_{\M}$, we can (1) efficiently identify all MECs within $\M$ that do not intersect with $G$ and include them in $B$, and (2) determine whether $\lambda^\ast$ is finite by verifying if $G$ is within the reachable set of $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-143: We have set two conditions for terminating the EVI algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-144: The first condition ($\|\tilde v_l-\tilde v_{l-1}\|_\infty\leq \frac{1 {2t_k}$) ensures that for every $s\in S$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:EVI-termination} \vpik(s)\leq\Lkt \vpik(s)+\frac{1}{2t_k},\end{equation} where $\Lkt$ is given by Eq.~\eqref{eq:belman-update .}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Further, we get for every $s\in S$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:EVI-termination-result} \vpik(s)+\frac{1}{2t_k}v^\ast(s)\geq0,\end{equation} where $v^\ast$ and $\vpik$ denote the vectors containing probabilities of reaching $G$ starting from different states, when policies $\Pitk$ and $\pi^\ast$ are followed on MDPs $\Mtk$ and $\Mp$, respectively (\cite{Jacksh2011 , Thm.~$7$).}
elements.sent-2411-146: Let $\Mtk'$ denote the MDP that is resulted by connecting $B$ to $s_\init$ with probability $1$ in $\Mtk$, and $\tautk\in \reals_{>0 ^{|S|}$ denote the vector whose entries contain the expected time to hit $G$ in $\Ptk'$ that is the DTMC induced by applying policy $\Pitk$ at MDP $\Mtk'$.}
elements.sent-2411-147: To compute $\tautk$, we need to solve the following linear system of equations: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lambda_K_LES} (\mathbb I - \Ptk')\tautk = u,\end{equation} where $u(s) = 0$ if $s\in G$ and $u(s) = 1$ otherwise.

elements.section-2411-005: Regret-Free Controller Synthesis for LTL Specifications
elements.sent-2411-185: In this section, we study the controller synthesis problem for MDPs with unknown (but fixed) transition function against LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-186: In the following, we state the main problem of study in this section.
elements.sent-2411-187: For a given LTL specification over an MDP, the corresponding optimal policy, in general, belongs to the class of (deterministic) non-positional policies, that are mappings from the finite paths over the MDP into the set of actions.
elements.sent-2411-188: In order to restrict the set of policies to Markovian (positional) policies, we need to compute the corresponding product MDP by taking the product between the MDP $\M$ and the automaton $\Ap$.
elements.sent-2411-189: Given an MDP $\M=(S,A,T,s_\init,AP)$ and DRA $\Ap =(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_\init, F)$ corresponding to an LTL formula $\p$, we denote the product MDP $\Mp=(\Sprod, \Aprod, \Tprod, \sinitprod, AP^\times)$, where $\Sprod=S\times Q$, $\Aprod=A$, $\sinitprod =(s_\init, q_\init)$, $AP^\times = AP \times Q$, and $\Tprod\colon \Sprod\mapsto \Delta(\Sprod)$ taking the form \begin{equation}\Tprod((s,q), a, (s',q'))= T(s,a,s') q'\in \delta(q, L(s')) 0 otherwise. \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-190: For the product MDP $\Mp$, we define accepting maximal end components (AMECs) as those MECs $G_j$, $j\in\{1,2,\dots,m\}$ in $\Mp$ for which $G_j= (S\times K_j)$.
elements.sent-2411-191: We denote the union of every AMEC in $\Mp$ by $G$.
elements.sent-2411-192: Likewise, the union of every non-accepting MEC $B_j$, $j\in\{1,2,\dots,m\}$ in $\Mp$ for which $B_j= (S\times J_j)$ in $\Mp$ is denoted as $B$.
elements.sent-2411-193: Let $\Mp=(\Sprod, \Aprod, \Tprod, \sinitprod, L, AP^\times)$ be the MDP computed as the product of the original MDP $\M$ and the corresponding DRA $\Ap$ that monitors progress of satisfaction for a given LTL specification $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-194: While the transition probability matrix $T$ is unknown, we can find the MECs within $\Mp$ through learning the graph $\chi_\M$ (see Alg.~\ref{alg:graph_learning ) and computing its product with the DRA $\Ap$.}
elements.sent-2411-195: In Sec.~\ref{app: graph-identification , we show a polynomial algorithm for learning the underlying graph of the MDP $\Mp$ using the knowledge of a positive lower bound over the minimum transition probabilities in $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Once we know the graph of $\Mp$, we can use Algorithm 47 from \cite{baier2008principles to characterize all of the MECs in $G$ and $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Alg.~\ref{alg:ltl-synthesis outlines our proposed online regret-free method for solving the controller synthesis problem against LTL specifications.}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Regret-free learning algorithm for general LTL specifications} \label{alg:ltl-synthesis} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} LTL specification $\p$, confidence parameter $\delta\in (0,1)$, state and action sets $S$ and $A$, connection graph $\chi_\M$ \STATE Compute a DRA $\Ap$ which accepts $\p$ \STATE Use the graph connection $\chi_\M$ and compute MECs $G$ and $B$ \STATE Run Alg.~\ref{alg:main} to compute and update the control policy over $\Mp$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-226: In this paper, we proposed a regret-free algorithm for the control synthesis problem over MDPs against infinite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-227: The defined regret quantifies the accumulated deviation over the probability of satisfying the given LTL specification.
elements.sent-2411-228: Below, we have discussed several aspects of the proposed scheme.
elements.sent-2411-229: Possibility of applying the regret-free algorithms that are proposed for SSP.
elements.sent-2411-230: The assumptions that are needed for solving SSP in a regret free way, are inherently non-applicable to our target setting as we discussed in the related work section.
elements.sent-2411-231: In particular, relaxing the requirement for existence of a proper policy (which is equivalent to the existence of a policy which satisfies the given LTL specification with probability one) requires attributing a large enough cost to the policies which may end up visiting the non-accepting MECs.
elements.sent-2411-232: Such an assumption (even in the case finding such an upper bound is feasible) would automatically require defining a cost structure over the product MDP which would in turn change the function of regret so that it can only be defined with respect to the accumulated cost and not the probability of satisfying the given specification.
elements.sent-2411-233: However, we desire knowing the value of regret with respect to the satisfaction probability and not any artificially defined accumulated cost.
elements.sent-2411-235: UCRL2 is a useful regret-free algorithm which is proposed for communicating MDPs with bounded diameter.
elements.sent-2411-236: Our setting is not appropriate for applying UCRL2 as the product MDP would not be communicating after all.
elements.sent-2411-237: However, it may be noticed that our second assumption, i.e., the knowledge over an upper bound for the expected hitting time of the optimal policy is a weaker assumption with respect to the main assumption for UCRL2 that is the knowledge over the diameter of the underlying MDP.. \bibliographystyle{icml2024 \onecolumn.}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Proposed algorithm for learning graphs} \label{alg:graph_learning} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} Initial state $s_\init$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}^{(t)}$, state and action sets $S$ and $A$. \STATE Compute $n^\ast$, that is minimum number of samples required to determine all outgoing transitions for any state-action pair $(s,a)\in S\times A$ with confidence $1-\delta_1$, assuming minimum transition probability $p_{min}$, \STATE $E\leftarrow \emptyset$ \STATE $t \leftarrow 0$ \FOR{$s\in S\setminus s_\init$} \STATE Compute a policy $\pi^{(s)}$ under which $s$ is reachable from $s_\init$ \FOR{$a\in A$} \STATE $n\leftarrow \#\{t'<t\colon s_t=s, a_t=a\}$ \WHILE{$n<n^\ast$} \STATE $s_t\leftarrow s_\init$ \STATE Run the transition from state-action pair $(s_t,\pi^{(s)}(s_t))$ and observe $s_{t+1}$ \IF{$s_t = s $} \STATE $n\leftarrow n+1$ \STATE break \ENDIF \STATE $t\leftarrow t+1$ \ENDWHILE \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-310: Learning to control an unknown dynamical system with respect to high-level temporal specifications is an important problem in control theory.
elements.sent-2411-311: We present the first regret-free online algorithm for learning a controller for linear temporal logic (LTL) specifications for systems with unknown dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-312: We assume that the underlying (unknown) dynamics is modeled by a finite-state and action Markov decision process (MDP).
elements.sent-2411-313: Our core technical result is a regret-free learning algorithm for infinite-horizon reach-avoid problems on MDPs.
elements.sent-2411-314: For general LTL specifications, we show that the synthesis problem can be reduced to a reach-avoid problem once the graph structure is known.
elements.sent-2411-315: Our LTL controller synthesis algorithm provides sharp bounds on how close we are to achieving optimal behavior after a finite number of learning episodes.
elements.sent-2411-316: In contrast, previous algorithms for LTL synthesis only provide asymptotic guarantees, which give no insight into the transient performance during the learning phase.
elements.sent-2411-317: We consider the problem of learning an optimal control policy for a stochastic system, whose dynamics are unknown, with respect to linear temporal logic (LTL) specifications.
elements.sent-2411-318: This is a core problem in control and robotics, with a rich body of existing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-319: A fundamental approach to the problem is to apply reinforcement learning (RL): the agent maintains a model of the world learned through exploration, and computes a sequence of approximations to an optimal control policy that maximizes the probability that the temporal specification is satisfied \cite{Icarte2018, camacho2019, hasanbeig2019, kazemi2022, hahn2019, Oura2020, Cai2020, Bozkurt2021, Sickert2016, alur2022framework, Fu2014, Chaudhuri:2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-320: In the limit, the approximations converge to the optimal policy.
elements.sent-2411-321: In practice, it is not enough to know that approximations converge in the limit; we would prefer to know how close our current policy is to the optimal one, and to stop learning when the policy is close to optimal.
elements.sent-2411-322: One way to quantify this is through regret minimization \cite{Jacksh2011, Agarwal2014, Srinivas2012, Dann2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-323: For an online learning algorithm, intuitively, regret is defined as the difference between the accumulated rewards collected by an optimal policy and the algorithm during learning.
elements.sent-2411-324: A learning algorithm is called regret-free if the regret grows sublinearly with the number of episodes.
elements.sent-2411-325: In this paper, we propose the first regret-free learning algorithm for policy synthesis against LTL objectives.
elements.sent-2411-326: We consider the class of systems whose dynamics can be captured by a finite-state and action Markov decision process (MDP) with unknown transition probabilities and fixed initial state $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-327: The objective is to synthesize a policy that maximizes the probability of satisfying a given LTL specification $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-328: Our online algorithm produces a sequence of policies $\pi_1,\pi_2,\dots$, one per episode.
elements.sent-2411-329: Let $v^\ast(s_\init)$ and $ v_k(s_\init)$ denote, respectively, the satisfaction probabilities of $\pi^\ast$ and $\pi_k$ when starting from $s_\init$.
elements.sent-2411-330: After passing $K>0$ episodes, we define the regret as $R(K)\coloneqq \sum_{k=1 ^K (v^\ast(s_\init) - v_k(s_\init))$, the accumulated difference between the satisfaction probabilities under the optimal and learned policy.}
elements.sent-2411-332: We first state our algorithm for reach-avoid specifications, the subset of LTL specifications that require a set of goal states must be visited while avoiding hitting a set of bad states.
elements.sent-2411-333: In every episode, our algorithm (1) computes an interval MDP (iMDP) by taking all the collected state observations into account; (2) finds an optimistic policy over the computed iMDP to solve the reach-avoid problem; and (3) executes the computed policy before an episode-specific deadline is reached.
elements.sent-2411-334: Next, for a general LTL specification, we show that the synthesis problem can be reduced to a reach-avoid problem if we know the graph structure of the MDP.
elements.sent-2411-335: Finally, we provide a polynomial algorithm for learning the graph structure of the MDP based only on having a positive lower bound on its non-zero transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-336: Fig.~\ref{fig:overview provides an overview of our proposed online policy learning algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Policy learning can be stopped after $k^\ast \geq 1$ episodes if the average regret $R(k^\ast)/k^\ast$ becomes sufficiently small, indicating that the learned policy's satisfaction probability is nearly optimal on average, with confidence at least $1 - \delta$.
elements.sent-2411-338: This can be verified using our proposed regret bounds.
elements.sent-2411-339: There exist regret-free algorithms for solving infinite-horizon policy synthesis problems, but they cannot be applied to our problem as we will discuss in more detail in Sec.~\ref{sec:related .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Our primary objective is to minimize the regret with respect to an optimal policy that maximizes the satisfaction probability of a specification.
elements.sent-2411-341: The regret for each episode is naturally bounded by one, which gives the regret bound of $\mathcal{O (K)$ for any arbitrary learning algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-342: To achieve a regret-free algorithm, we define an episode-specific deadline that categorizes learning episodes as fast or slow.
elements.sent-2411-343: We prove that the number of slow episodes exceeding the deadline grows sublinearly.
elements.sent-2411-344: The deadline is set such that the regret from fast episodes also grows sublinearly.
elements.sent-2411-345: Thus, the total regret of our algorithm increases sublinearly, making it regret-free.
elements.sent-2411-346: We utilize a reset mechanism that enables a systematic trade-off between exploration and exploitation, and can be applied to non-communicating MDPs.
elements.sent-2411-347: This is unlike the previous work by \citet{Tarbouriech:2019 , which is limited to strictly positive cost structures and properties with satisfaction probability one, and surpasses the limitations of UCRL2-like algorithms, which require the underlying MDP to be communicating.}
elements.sent-2411-348: The ability to handle non-communicating MDPs is key since the product of an MDP and an automaton for the temporal specification makes in general the product MDP to be non-communicating.
elements.sent-2411-349: We have implemented our algorithm with empirical evaluation on reach-avoid specifications in a gridworld environment reported in App.~\ref{sec:experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-350: In particular, we compare the performance and convergence of our algorithm to the sample complexity and convergence of a PAC-MDP algorithm for LTL \cite{Mateo:2023 , and show that our algorithm convergences faster within a smaller number of episodes.}
elements.sent-2411-351: Reinforcement Learning for LTL Specifications Without Guarantees: In recent years, substantial research has focused on the policy synthesis problem for systems modeled as finite MDPs with unknown transition probabilities, where the objective is to satisfy tasks specified by LTL formulas.
elements.sent-2411-352: Early results focused only on finite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-353: The development of good-for-MDP automata, such as limit deterministic B\"uchi automata (LDBA), for representing LTL formulas has led to significant advances in reinforcement learning for the full class of LTL specifications, including the infinite-horizon ones \cite{Sickert2016,hahn2020good,kazemi2022,hasanbeig2023certified .}
elements.sent-2411-354: Typically, one has to first translate the given LTL formula into an appropriate automaton, such as an LDBA, and then compute the product of this automaton with the MDP to formulate the final (discounted) learning problem.
elements.sent-2411-355: This formulation ensures that, with a sufficiently large discount factor (depending on system dynamics and the specification), applying standard RL algorithms will lead the policy to converge asymptotically to the optimal one \cite{kazemi2022, Bozkurt2021, Oura2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-356: Translation of the LTL formula to an average objective for RL using the automaton construction is also studied by \citet{kazemi2025average .}
elements.sent-2411-357: However, these methods do not provide a finite-time performance, and the required discount factor is not known in advance.
elements.sent-2411-358: Reinforcement Learning for LTL Specifications With Guarantees: The two most popular metrics for evaluating the performance of learning algorithms are probably approximately correct (PAC) and regret bounds.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, the sample complexity of the algorithm scales explicitly with the horizon length, rendering it unsuitable for infinite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-360: Subsequently, a surprising negative result was established: full LTL is not PAC-learnable \cite{yang2021tractability, alur2022framework .}
elements.sent-2411-361: A closer analysis reveals that this result primarily stems from the assumption of an unknown minimum transition probability.
elements.sent-2411-362: By instead assuming a known minimum transition probability, \citet{Chaudhuri:2023 proposed a PAC-learnable policy synthesis method that relies on access to a generative model capable of sampling from any arbitrary state-action pair.}
elements.sent-2411-363: Our proposed algorithm does not require access to a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-364: Recently, \citet{Mateo:2023 have also proposed another PAC learning algorithm that does not rely on a generative model.}
elements.sent-2411-365: However, there is still no regret-free online algorithm for policy synthesis to satisfy LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-366: Regret-Free Reinforcement Learning for Communicating MDPs: UCRL and UCRL2 are well-known regret-free learning algorithms developed for communicating MDPs \cite{Jacksh2011, Auer2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-367: The definition of regret in the context of communicating MDPs is particularly well-suited to our objective: it is evaluated over an infinite sequence of states without discounting future observations, which aligns closely with the requirements of infinite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-368: A key advantage of learning in communicating MDPs is that it can proceed indefinitely without requiring environment resets.
elements.sent-2411-369: However, in the setting we consider, even if the underlying MDP is communicating, its product with the automaton representing the specification may result in a non-communicating MDP \cite{kazemi2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-370: In contrast, our setting assumes a fixed initial state from a transient subset, rendering algorithms like UCRL2--which are tailored for communicating MDPs--inapplicable.
elements.sent-2411-371: Regret-Free Reinforcement Learning for Non-Communicating MDPs: Goal-oriented reinforcement learning is a key class of problems in RL, often formulated as a shortest path problem (SPP) for MDPs with unknown transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-372: Recent theoretical results include the works by \citet{Tarbouriech:2019 and \citet{rosenberg2020 .}}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Regret-free algorithm for until formulas ($\text{ZeroReg}$)} \label{alg:main} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} State and action sets $S$ and $A$, initial state $s_\init$, sets $G$ and $B$, confidence parameter $\delta\in(0,1)$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}$ \STATE Initialization: Set $t=1$, $s_1=s_\init$ \FOR{episodes $k=1,2,\dots$} \STATE { Construct iMDP $\Mbk$:} \STATE $t_k\leftarrow t$ \STATE Set $N_k(s,a)\coloneqq |\{t<t_k\colon s_t=s, a_t=a\}|$ \STATE For all $s,s'\in S$ and $a\in A$ compute the empirical transition function \begin{equation}\Thk(s'| s,a)\coloneqq\frac{|\{t<t_k\colon s_t=s, a_t=a, s_{t+1}=s'\}|}{\max\{1,N_k(s,a)\}}\end{equation} \STATE Define the iMDP $\Mbk=(S, A, \Tbk, s_\init, L, AP)$ with the set of transition functions $\Tbk$ satisfying Eq.~\eqref{eq:trans-conf-bound} \STATE { Compute policy $\Pitk$:} \STATE Use Alg.~\ref{alg:EVI} to find an optimistic MDP $\Mtk$ and a policy $\Pitk$, i.e., $(\Mtk, \Pitk) = \text{EVI}(S,A,G,B,\Tbk,t_k, p_{min})$ \STATE { Execute policy $\Pitk$:} \STATE Compute the deadline $H_k$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq:deadline-def} \WHILE{$s_t\notin G$ and $(t-t_k)\leq H_k$} \IF{$s_t\notin B$} \STATE Observe the next state $s_{t+1}$ by executing $\Pitk(s_t)$ \ELSE \STATE $s_{t+1}\leftarrow s_{\init}$ \ENDIF \STATE $t\leftarrow t+1$ \ENDWHILE \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-007: \caption{Extended value iteration ($\text{EVI}$)} \label{alg:EVI} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} State and action sets $S$ and $A$, sets $G$ and $B$, set of plausible transition functions $\Tbk$, start time of the $k^{th}$ episode $t_k$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}$ \STATE Set $l=0$, $\tilde \mu_0(s)=0$ for $s\notin G$ and $\tilde \mu_0(s)=1$ for $s\in G$ \REPEAT \STATE $l\leftarrow l+1$ \FOR{$s \in S$} \FOR{$a \in A$} \STATE $\Ttk(.|s,a) = \text{InnerMax}(s,a,\Tbk,\tilde \mu_{l-1})$ \ENDFOR \STATE $\Pitk(s) = \argmax_{a \in A} \sum_{s'\in S}\Ttk(s'|s,a)\tilde \mu_{l-1}(s')$ \STATE $\tilde \mu_l(s) = \sum_{s'\in S}\Ttk(s'|s,\Pitk(s))\tilde \mu_{l-1}(s')$ \ENDFOR \UNTIL{$\|\tilde \mu_l-\tilde \mu_{l-1}\|_\infty< \min(\frac{1}{2t_k}, p_{min}^{|S|})$} \STATE Construct the MDP $\Mtk=(S,A,\Ttk,s_\init,L,AP)$ \STATE { Results:} $(\Mtk, \Pitk) = \text{EVI}(S, A, G,B,\Tbk,t_k, p_{min})$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{Computing optimistic MDP in Eq.~\eqref{eq:belman-update} ($\text{InnerMax}$)} \label{alg:innermax} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Inputs:} State-action pair $(s,a)$, interval transition function $\Tbk$, and value vector $\tilde \mu_l$ \STATE Compute $\Thk(.| s,a)$ and $d(s,a)$ as the center and $\ell_1$ radius of $\Tbk(.|s,a)$, respectively \STATE Sort the states according to their value in $\tilde \mu_l$, and get $S'=\{s_1',\dots,s_n'\}$ with $\tilde \mu_l(s_1')\geq\tilde \mu_l(s_2')\geq\dots\geq\tilde \mu_l(s_n')$ \STATE Compute the vector $\hat p$ as $\qquad\hat p(s_1') = \Thk(s,a,s_1')+d(s,a)/2$, $\qquad\hat p(s_j') = \Thk(s,a,s_j')$ for $j\in\{2,3,\ldots,n\}$ \STATE $j\leftarrow n$ \WHILE{$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\hat p(s_i')>1$} \STATE $\hat p(s_j')=\max\{0,1-\sum_{i=1,i\neq j}^{n}\hat p(s_i')\}$ \STATE $j\leftarrow j-1$ \ENDWHILE \STATE Set $\Ttk(s,a,s_j')=\hat p(s_j')$ for $j\in\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$. \STATE { Results:} $\Ttk(.| s, a)= \text{InnerMax}(s,a,\Tbk,\tilde \mu_l)$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-489: In this section, we study the policy synthesis problem for MDPs with an unknown transition function against LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-490: Given an LTL formula $\p$, one can construct a deterministic Rabin automaton (DRA) whose language corresponds to all infinite sequences of system behaviors satisfying $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-491: This automaton, denoted by $\Ap = (Q, \Sigma, \gamma, q_\init, F)$, consists of a finite set of states $Q$, a finite alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{\AP}$, a transition function $\gamma\colon Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$, an initial state $q_\init$, and an accepting condition $F=\{(J_i,K_i)\mid i=1,\dots,m\}$, consisting of subsets $J_i$ and $K_i$ of $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-492: An infinite sequence $\sigma$ is accepted by $\Ap$ if there exists at least one pair $(J, K) \in F$ such that $\inf(\sigma) \cap J = \emptyset$ and $\inf(\sigma) \cap K \neq \emptyset$, where $\inf(\sigma)$ is the set of states that appear infinitely often in $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-493: For a given LTL specification and an MDP, the corresponding optimal policy, in general, belongs to the class of (deterministic) non-positional policies, that are mappings from the finite paths over the MDP into the set of actions.
elements.sent-2411-494: One can restrict the set of policies to positional policies by constructing the corresponding product MDP, obtained as the product between the MDP $\M$ and the automaton $\Ap$.
elements.sent-2411-495: Given an MDP $\M=(S,A,T,s_\init,L,\AP)$ and a DRA $\Ap =(Q, \Sigma, \gamma, q_\init, F)$, we denote the product MDP by the tuple $\Mp=(\Sprod, \Aprod, \Tprod, \sinitprod, \Lprod, \AP^\times)$, where $\Sprod=S\times Q$, $\Aprod=A$, $\sinitprod =(s_\init, q_\init)$, $\AP^\times = \AP$, $\Lprod\colon (s,q)\mapsto L(s)$ for every $(s,q)\in S\times Q$, and $\Tprod\colon \Sprod\rightarrow \Delta(\Sprod)$ taking the form \begin{equation}\Tprod((s,q), a, (s',q'))= T(s,a,s') q'= \gamma(q, L(s')) 0 otherwise. \end{equation} In App.~\ref{app: graph-identification , we show a polynomial algorithm for learning the underlying graph of the MDP $\M$, i.e., $\chi(\M)$, using the knowledge of $p_{min}$ (see Alg.~\ref{alg:graph_learning ).}}
elements.sent-2411-496: Knowledge of $\chi(\M)$ directly gives the graph for the product MDP $\Mp$, that is $\chi(\Mp)=(\Sprod, \Aprod, \Eprod)$, where $((s,q), a, (s',q'))\in \Eprod$ if and only if \begin{equation}\label{eq:graph_prod_mdp} ((s,q), a, (s',q'))\in \Eprod\Leftrightarrow \nonumber (s,a,s')\in E  and  q'\in \gamma(q,L(s')).\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-009: \caption{Regret-free learning algorithm for general LTL specifications} \label{alg:ltl-synthesis} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} State and action sets $S$ and $A$, initial state $s_\init$, LTL specification $\p$, confidence parameter $\delta\in (0,1)$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}\in(0,1)$ \STATE Construct a DRA $\Ap=(Q, \Sigma, \gamma, q_\init, F)$ which accepts $\p$ \STATE Run Alg.~\ref{alg:graph_learning} to get the connection graph $\chi(\M)$, i.e., $\chi(\M)=\mathsf{GraphLearn}(S,A, s_\init, \delta/2, p_{min})$ \STATE Compute the graph $\chi(\Mp)$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq:graph_prod_mdp} \STATE Compute MECs within $\Mp$ using Alg.~47 in \cite{baier2008principles} \STATE $\Gprod\leftarrow \emptyset$ \STATE $\Bprod\leftarrow \emptyset$ \FOR{MEC $C$ inside $\Mp$ with state space $D$} \STATE Use Eq.~\eqref{eq:AMEC_def_prod_MDP} to check if $C$ is an AMEC \IF{$C$ is an AMEC} \STATE $\Gprod\leftarrow \Gprod\cup D$ \ELSE \STATE $\Bprod\leftarrow \Bprod\cup D$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE Run Alg.~\ref{alg:main} with state and action sets $\Sprod$ and $\Aprod$, initial state $\sinitprod$, sets $\Gprod$ and $\Bprod$, confidence parameter $\delta/2$, and minimum transition probability $p_{min}$, to compute and update the policy over $\Mp$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-505: In this paper, we proposed a regret-free algorithm for the control policy synthesis problem over unknown MDPs against infinite-horizon LTL specifications.
elements.sent-2411-506: The defined regret quantifies the accumulated deviation from the optimal probability of satisfying the LTL specification.
elements.sent-2411-507: We first propose a regret-free algorithm for until (reach-avoid) formulas, and then extend this approach to solve the synthesis problem for general LTL specifications by leveraging the knowledge of a minimum transition probability.
elements.sent-2411-508: This probability is used to design an efficient graph-learning algorithm with arbitrary precision.

elements.algorithm-2411-010: \caption{Graph learning algorithm ($\text{GraphLearn}$)} \label{alg:graph_learning} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} State and action sets $S$ and $A$, initial state $s_\init$, confidence $\delta\in(0,1)$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}\in(0,1)$ \STATE Initialization: $t \leftarrow 1$, $E\leftarrow \emptyset$, $n(s,a)=0$ for every $(s,a)\in S\times A$, and $N(s,a,s')=0$ for every $(s,a,s')\in S\times A\times S$ \STATE Compute a lower bound for $n^\ast$ using Eq.~\eqref{graph_sample_complexity} \STATE Set $L\coloneqq |S|\frac{\log(\delta/6)}{\log(1 - \pmin^{|S|})}$ \STATE { Compute policies:} \FOR{$s\in S\setminus s_\init$} \STATE Compute a policy $\pi^{(s)}$ under which $s$ is reachable from $s_\init$ with positive probability: $\pi^{(s)} = \text{Reach}(S, A, s,, \delta, p_{min})$ \ENDFOR \FOR{$(s,a)\in S\times A$} \STATE $\pi^{(s)}(s) \leftarrow a$ \STATE { Collect enough samples by executing the computed policies:} \WHILE{$n(s,a)<n^\ast$} \STATE $s_t\leftarrow s_\init$ \FOR{$1\leq t \leq L$} \STATE Execute the action $a_t=\pi^{(s)}(s_t)$ and observe the next state $s_{t+1}$ \STATE $n(s_t,a_t)\leftarrow n(s_t,a_t)+1$ \STATE $N(s_t,a_t,s_{t+1})\leftarrow N(s_t,a_t,s_{t+1})+1$ \STATE $t\leftarrow t+1$ \IF{$s_t = s $} \STATE break \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \STATE { Verify existence of transitions:} \FOR{$s'\in S$} \IF{$N(s,a,s')>0$} \STATE $E\leftarrow E\cup \set{(s,a,s')}$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE { Results:} $\chi(\M) = \text{GraphLearn}(S, A, s_\init, \delta, p_{min})$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-011: \caption{Reachability policy synthesis for graph learning ($\text{Reach}$)} \label{alg:omega_PAC_reachability} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Inputs:} State and action sets $S$ and $A$, target state $s^\ast\in S\setminus \{s_\init\}$, confidence parameter $\delta\in (0,1)$, minimum transition probability $p_{min}\in (0,1)$ \STATE $n(s,a)\leftarrow 0$ for every $(s,a)\in S\times A$ \STATE $N(s,a,s')\leftarrow 0$ for every $(s,a,s')\in S\times A\times S$ \STATE Compute $C_3$ and $H$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq:omega_pac_constant} \STATE $G\leftarrow \set{s^\ast}$ \WHILE{True} \FOR{$(s,a)\in S\times A$} \IF{$n(s,a)<c$} \STATE $\hat{T}(s,a,s')=\begin{cases}1 \text{if }s'=s 0 \text{Otherwise}\end{cases}$ \STATE $G\leftarrow G\cup \set{s}$ \ELSE \STATE $\hat{T}(s,a,s')=\frac{N'(s,a,s')}{n'(s,a)}$ \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $\hat M=(S,A,\hat T, s_\init, \hat L, \hat {AP})$, with $\hat {AP}=\{\Goal\}$, and $\hat L(s^\ast)=\set{\Goal}$ and $\hat L(s)=\emptyset$ if $s\neq s^\ast$ \STATE Compute optimal policy $\pi^{(s^\ast)}$ for $\hat \M$ by solving the corresponding linear program (see~Sec.~$10.6.1$ in \cite{baier2008principles}) \STATE For $\hat \M$, compute $S_{H}\subseteq S$ as the set of states that are reachable from $s_{\init}$ within $2H$ time steps \IF{$n(s,a)\geq C_3$ for every $(s,a)\in S_H\times A$} \STATE break \ENDIF \STATE $s_1\leftarrow s_\init$ \FOR{$1\leq t \leq 2H$} \STATE Execute the action $a_t=\pi^{(s^\ast)}(s_t)$ and observe the next state $s_{t+1}$ \STATE $n(s_t,a_t)\leftarrow n(s_t,a_t)+1$ \STATE $N(s_t,a_t,s_{t+1})\leftarrow N(s_t,a_t,s_{t+1})+1$ \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \STATE { Results:} $\pi^{(s^\ast)} = \text{Reach}(S, A, s^\ast, \delta, p_{min})$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-616: We considered a reach-avoid policy synthesis problem in the gridworld example described in Fig.~\ref{fig:gridworld .}
elements.sent-2411-617: The world is characterized by $l\in\nats_{\geq 4}$ that denotes the number of cells per column and row.
elements.sent-2411-618: We have conducted experiments to (1) evaluate the empirical performance of our algorithcm, (2) observe how episode length vary throughout the run of our algorithm, and (3) assess the sample complexity of our method.
elements.sent-2411-619: Empirical performance: Fig.~\ref{fig:regret_growth illustrates the variations of empirical mean for the normalized regret $R(K)/K$ for our regret-free algorithm which is run for the gridworld example with $l=6$.}
elements.sent-2411-620: The considered specification is $\p = \neg\Unsafe \LTLuntil \Goal$, where $G$ is marked as $\Goal$ and walls are marked as $\Unsafe$.
elements.sent-2411-621: It can be observed that the empirical mean of the regret drops very quickly, which implies that the algorithm successfully finds an optimal policy within the few first episodes.
elements.sent-2411-622: We also compare the performance of our proposed method with the $\omega$-PAC algorithm of \cite{Mateo:2023 , which is the only existing method that supports guaranteed policy synthesis against infinite-horizon temporal specifications.}
elements.sent-2411-623: The $\omega$-PAC algorithm takes a confidence parameter $\delta\in(0,1)$ and a precision parameter $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ and provides a policy which has $\varepsilon$-optimal satisfaction probability with confidence $(1-\delta)$.
elements.sent-2411-624: We believe that this is because our algorithm uses the intermediate confidence bounds, while the $\omega$-PAC algorithm waits until enough many samples are collected, and only then updates its policy.
elements.sent-2411-625: Episode length variations: Fig.~\ref{fig:deadline_changes illustrates the variations in $H_k$ for different episodes.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Initially, our algorithm assigns very small values to $H_k$, since the expected time to reach $G$ in the optimistic MDP is small.
elements.sent-2411-627: As the empirical transition probabilities become more precise, the estimation over the expected time to reach $G$ takes more accurate values.
